items.q: Which type of nuclear radiation is most ionising?
items.correct: Alpha radiation — large, doubly charged particles cause the most ionisation per unit distance.
items.misconception: Gamma is most ionising because it can travel furthest and pass through almost anything.
items.random_wrong: Beta radiation is most ionising because it consists of fast electrons.
items.why: Students conflate penetrating power with ionising power — gamma penetrates most because it ionises least, the opposite of intuition.

items.q: Which of the following is a fundamental particle: proton, electron, or neutron?
items.correct: Only the electron — protons and neutrons are made of quarks.
items.misconception: All three (proton, electron, neutron) are fundamental particles that cannot be broken down.
items.random_wrong: Only the proton is fundamental; electrons and neutrons are composite.
items.why: The atomic model is introduced with proton/neutron/electron as building blocks, so students don't update when quarks appear in later courses.

items.correct: No — its photon energy is below the threshold required to ionise atoms; ionising radiation includes UV (high energy), X-rays and gamma.
items.misconception: Yes — all electromagnetic radiation is ionising because it carries energy.
items.random_wrong: Yes — only infrared and radio waves are non-ionising.
items.why: Students don't link photon energy (E = hf) to the ability to ionise, so they treat all EM radiation as equivalent in damage potential.

items.q: A car travels 4 km north then 3 km east. What is its displacement magnitude?
items.why: It's 5 km. “7 km” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which of the following is a scalar quantity?
items.why: It's Kinetic energy. “Acceleration” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
